claims.text: Applying pure logic in business strategy is disadvantageous because it converges on the same solutions as competitors, creating a race to the bottom. Instead, identify where competitors' logic is too narrow and restrictive, then exploit the gaps in their model.
claims.mechanism: When all competitors use identical logic, they arrive at identical positions and compete on the same dimensions, eliminating differentiation.

claims.text: Competitors using standard logic (e.g., seeking proximity to tube lines when house hunting in London) optimize locally within popular information frameworks. Exploiting a competitor's logical gap means using a different information source entirely (e.g., rail maps instead of tube maps) to access cheaper assets with superior outcomes (lower cost, faster commute time).
conditions: Competitors optimizing using publicly available frameworks
Information asymmetry exists (alternate data sources available but underutilized)
claims.mechanism: Competitors' logic is constrained by shared assumptions about what data to optimize against; switching to an orthogonal constraint axis (rail vs. tube) unlocks superior trade-offs unavailable when locked into the dominant framework.
bounded_by: Does not apply when all information sources are equally accessible and competed upon

examples.text: In London housing, conventional logic directs buyers to locate near tube stations because the tube map is universally available and accessible to all. A buyer using a rail map instead identifies railway stations as equally viable transit, accessing cheaper properties in less-competed areas while achieving faster commute times than buyers who followed the logic of the majority.

practices.text: Find the publicly available information source your competitors are all using (the shared logical framework). Identify an orthogonal data source that provides superior outcomes on your valued dimensions.
Test whether competitors' logic is actually optimal or merely popular; explore alternative frameworks that competitors have excluded from their reasoning.
